name: proof-guided-web-change-app
notes: The app contains two private workflows: a deterministic durable-setting repair and a bounded primary-CTA agent change.

Both workflows use one generic controller for serialized one-shot attempts, immutable candidate history, new revisions after changes, and fresh attempts after unusable checks; contracts, candidate resolution, and change policies remain workflow-owned.
Each workflow pins its expected contract inside the application and accepts only app-issued opaque candidate references from ordinary callers.
The owned specimen servers bind only to ephemeral 127.0.0.1 ports.

The shell and each specimen require ephemeral 256-bit run capabilities; proof-target authorization is injected into the Playwright context and excluded from semantic targets, signed evidence, audit identities, and retained artifacts.
Each workflow owns exactly two executable specimen source variants, and visible display runtimes are separate from fresh proof-target runtimes.
The durable-setting executor receives immutable source bytes, the displayed task, and meaning-level findings, never proof audit material.

The CTA agent protocol exposes only a proposal reference, base-source digest, displayed task, meaning-level findings, current and requested CTA values, and one permitted mutation kind; it exposes no contract, profile, proof authority, signing key, run capability, proof transport, disposition, or audit record.

The CTA application accepts only an exact, base-bound proposal for the pinned CTA values, reconstructs the exact reviewed source itself, and records a separate proposal audit only after the resulting repair is committed.

The subprocess CTA adapter requires an absolute executable, an explicit real non-symlink working directory, shell:false, an empty environment by default or explicit string-only environment, bounded output, and a timeout.
The subprocess controls are not an operating-system sandbox; the child retains every filesystem, network, and subprocess capability granted to its user and executable by the operating system.

The local runtime owns loopback networking, private artifact filesystem access, ephemeral signing, specimen execution, and the Playwright subprocess; browser artifacts may contain sensitive rendered content and must remain in an approved private directory.
Retained artifact directories and files are permission-restricted, private signing keys remain process-local, and portable receipts use relative rather than workstation paths.
The application has no hosted Riddle client, configuration, or request path.
